main_ingredients: salad dressing (olive oil vinaigrette or Caesar dressing)
allergens: may contain fish (anchovies in Caesar dressing)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of grilled chicken salad is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 100–120 g of grilled chicken breast and 180–200 g of mixed greens, vegetables, and dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Grill Salad — HPB Singapore Foodo Database (G00010814)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record G00010814 for Chicken Grill Salad. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed salad with protein and oil-based dressing
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Grill Salad. GI estimated at approximately 32 based on analogous mixed green salad with grilled protein and oil-based dressing entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The low carbohydrate content (5.9 g/100g), high fat (44.54 g/100g from dressing), and protein content collectively suppress glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (32) × carbohydrate per serving (17.7 g for 300 g portion) / 100 = 5.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 810 mg/100g — well above the 600 mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request dressing on the side and use sparingly, choose olive oil and vinegar over Caesar dressing, and ask for minimal seasoning salt on the chicken, as the 810 mg/100g sodium content (equivalent to approximately 2,430 mg sodium per 300 g serving) substantially exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g) and low sugar (4.69 g/100g) with high fat from olive oil dressing supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken grilled salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate load (17.7 g per 300 g serving) and estimated low GI of 32, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 5.7 per serving; however, patients should choose olive oil vinaigrette over Caesar dressing to avoid added sugars and excess sodium, and avoid croutons or bread accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (44.54 g/100g) from dressing with elevated sodium (810 mg/100g); cholesterol at 26 mg/100g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the fat in this dish is predominantly unsaturated (olive oil vinaigrette), the very high total fat content of 44.54 g/100g and sodium of 810 mg/100g are concerns for cardiovascular disease patients; individuals should request dressing on the side, limit portion size, and confirm dressing type to ensure MUFA-dominant fat profile rather than saturated-fat-rich Caesar dressing.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFA from olive oil vinaigrette offset by high sodium (810 mg/100g) which may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled chicken salad with olive oil vinaigrette provides anti-inflammatory MUFA and oleocanthal polyphenols beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; however, the high sodium content (810 mg/100g) may exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling, and nightshade-sensitive patients should request removal of tomatoes and bell peppers.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Grilled Salad is a Western-style composed salad dish served in Singapore, consisting of succulent grilled chicken breast placed over a bed of fresh mixed greens such as romaine lettuce, spinach, and rocket, accompanied by cherry tomatoes, cucumber, and bell pepper, and dressed with an olive oil vinaigrette or Caesar dressing. The chicken is marinated and grilled, producing a lightly charred, juicy protein component with a smoky flavour. The salad is crisp and refreshing in texture, with the dressing providing richness and acidity. Per 100 g, the dish provides 430 kcal, 1.32 g protein, 44.54 g fat (predominantly from the dressing), and 5.9 g carbohydrate with 4.69 g sugar. The high fat content reflects the generous application of oil-based dressing rather than the chicken itself. Sodium is notably high at 810 mg/100g, primarily from the dressing and seasoning, making this dish unsuitable for hypertension patients without modification. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI ~32) and is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed with olive oil vinaigrette and without croutons. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner option in Western-style cafes and restaurants across Singapore. The dish is halal-friendly when prepared with appropriate seasoning and dressing, and is naturally gluten-free when croutons are omitted.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chicken grill salad?
Is chicken salad good for diabetes?
Can I eat grilled chicken salad if I have high blood pressure?
What is the sodium content of chicken grill salad?
Is grilled chicken salad low carb?
What dressing is used in chicken grill salad?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00010814 with confidence 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed with provenance canonical. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this composite dish.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record. This is likely underreported given the vegetable content of the dish (mixed greens, tomatoes, cucumber, bell pepper); actual dietary fibre from fresh vegetables would typically contribute 1–3 g/100g. The value 0.0 is retained as reported by the approved source (HPB G00010814) and flagged as a known limitation. This is a confirmed zero per the HPB source, not a naturally zero value — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010814. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo record G00010814; not available in the Singapore HPB database for this composite dish as of 2026. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in HPB Foodo record G00010814 for this composite dish. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on mixed green salad with grilled protein and oil-based dressing from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. The description references South American flavors in the input; this likely refers to the marinade style and is retained in context but the dish is classified as Western cuisine as served in Singapore. 6. Protein at 1.32 g/100g is notably low for a chicken-based dish and likely reflects the high dressing-to-chicken ratio in the HPB composite measurement; this value is retained as reported by the approved source.
